_comment: JCPOA conflict graph — Complete Tier 3 example with all 15 node types and all 20 edge types

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: Islamic Republic of Iran
nodes.description: The Islamic Republic of Iran, a theocratic republic governed by the Supreme Leader and elected institutions. Pursuing civilian nuclear energy and, according to Western assessments, nuclear weapons capability. Central party to the JCPOA negotiations.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: United States of America
nodes.description: The United States, a liberal democracy and global hegemon. Primary driver of sanctions pressure on Iran and architect of the JCPOA alongside the EU3. Withdrew from the JCPOA under the Trump administration in 2018.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: Russian Federation
nodes.description: Russia, a permanent UN Security Council member and signatory to the JCPOA. Has commercial interests in Iranian nuclear program (Bushehr reactor) and strategic interest in limiting US regional dominance.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: People's Republic of China
nodes.description: China, a permanent UN Security Council member and JCPOA signatory. Major importer of Iranian oil and proponent of multipolarity. Plays a significant role in JCPOA enforcement architecture.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: United Kingdom
nodes.description: The United Kingdom, a permanent UN Security Council member, EU3 member, and co-negotiator of the JCPOA. Maintains sanctions coordination through E3 format post-Brexit.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: French Republic
nodes.description: France, a permanent UN Security Council member, EU3 member, and co-negotiator of the JCPOA. Often takes a hawkish position on Iranian compliance within European diplomacy.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: Federal Republic of Germany
nodes.description: Germany, EU3 member and co-negotiator of the JCPOA. The largest European economy with significant commercial interests in Iran and a strong commitment to non-proliferation.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: European Union / High Representative
nodes.description: The European Union, represented by the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy. Served as the coordinator and chair of the JCPOA negotiations (E3+3 format) and custodian of INSTEX.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: International Atomic Energy Agency
nodes.description: The IAEA, the UN body responsible for nuclear safeguards and verification. Central to JCPOA implementation: IAEA inspectors verify Iranian compliance with nuclear commitments under the Additional Protocol.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: State of Israel
nodes.description: Israel, a nuclear-armed state and regional power that views an Iranian nuclear capability as an existential threat. Not a party to the JCPOA but a significant external actor that has conducted covert operations against Iran's nuclear program.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
nodes.description: Saudi Arabia, a Sunni Arab monarchy and Iran's primary regional rival. Deeply concerned about Iranian nuclear capability and regional hegemony. Not a party to JCPOA but exerts political pressure on the US and maintains separate enrichment ambitions.

nodes.label: Actor
nodes.name: Hezbollah
nodes.description: Hezbollah, a Lebanese Shia political movement and militia that is Iran's primary regional proxy. Possesses a large precision missile arsenal. Iran's support for Hezbollah is a key grievance of Israel and Gulf states in JCPOA negotiations.

nodes.label: Conflict
nodes.name: Iran Nuclear Program Dispute
nodes.summary: A long-running international dispute over Iran's nuclear program. The core incompatibility is between Iran's assertion of its right to civilian nuclear enrichment and Western/Israeli insistence that Iranian enrichment capacity must be constrained to prevent weapons breakout. The JCPOA of 2015 temporarily resolved the standoff, but US withdrawal in 2018 and subsequent Iranian non-compliance have returned the conflict to a stalemate phase with elevated glasl dynamics.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: Geneva interim agreement (Joint Plan of Action, JPOA) signed between Iran and the P5+1. Iran agreed to freeze parts of its nuclear program in exchange for limited sanctions relief. First major diplomatic breakthrough after years of failed talks.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) signed in Vienna between Iran and the P5+1 (US, UK, France, Germany, Russia, China) plus EU. Iran agreed to dramatic reductions in its enrichment capacity, redesign of Arak reactor, and IAEA Additional Protocol access in exchange for lifting nuclear-related sanctions.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: US President Trump announced US withdrawal from the JCPOA and reimposition of secondary sanctions against Iran under the 'maximum pressure' campaign. European signatories remained in the deal and attempted to create INSTEX payment mechanism to shield trade from US sanctions.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: Iran began exceeding JCPOA limits on enriched uranium stockpiles as leverage to pressure European signatories into meaningful sanctions relief. Iran progressively reduced its JCPOA commitments in five stages, citing US non-compliance and European failure to deliver economic benefits.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport killed IRGC-Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani. Iran announced it would no longer be bound by any JCPOA operational limits. Severe escalation in US-Iran tensions with Iran firing ballistic missiles at US bases in Iraq.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: Indirect negotiations between the US and Iran in Vienna to restore the JCPOA, mediated by the EU. Eight rounds of talks through 2021–2022 produced a near-final agreement text but broke down over Iran's demand that the IRGC be removed from the US terrorist designation list.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: IAEA confirmed Iran had enriched uranium to 83.7% purity — near weapons-grade — at Fordow facility. Combined with 60% enriched stockpile, Iran's breakout time had shrunk to approximately two weeks. Severe escalation in proliferation risk.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.description: UK, France, and Germany (E3) triggered the JCPOA snapback mechanism at the UN Security Council, reimposing all pre-2015 UN sanctions on Iran. Iran denounced the action as illegal and threatened further nuclear escalation.

nodes.label: Issue
nodes.name: Iranian Enrichment Rights
nodes.description: The central substantive incompatibility: Iran asserts an inalienable NPT right to civilian uranium enrichment on its territory; the US and P3 insist that Iranian enrichment must be constrained to minimal levels given proliferation risk.

nodes.label: Issue
nodes.name: Comprehensive Sanctions Relief
nodes.description: Iran demands complete removal of all US and EU nuclear-related sanctions, including secondary sanctions that deter third-party trade. The US insists on maintaining leverage through phased, conditional sanctions relief tied to verified compliance.

nodes.label: Issue
nodes.name: IRGC Foreign Terrorist Organisation Designation
nodes.description: Iran demands removal of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps from the US Foreign Terrorist Organisation list as a condition of JCPOA restoration. The US and Congress have resisted removal due to IRGC regional activities. Near-zero divisibility — treated as a domestic political constraint by both sides.

nodes.label: Issue
nodes.name: Iranian Regional Proxy Activities
nodes.description: US, Israel, and Gulf states demand constraints on Iran's ballistic missile program and support for regional proxies (Hezbollah, Hamas, Houthi). Iran insists these are non-nuclear issues outside JCPOA scope and constitutive of its strategic deterrence.

nodes.label: Interest
nodes.description: Iran's core security interest: regime survival and deterrence against US-led or Israeli military attack. The nuclear program, whether civilian or weapons-capable, is perceived as the ultimate deterrent against regime change.
nodes.stated_position: Iran is pursuing civilian nuclear energy as its sovereign right under the NPT
nodes.batna_description: Continue maximum enrichment under economic pressure while building deterrence through proxy network

nodes.label: Interest
nodes.description: Iran's economic interest: access to international financial system, oil exports, and foreign investment to address severe inflation, unemployment, and economic contraction caused by sanctions.
nodes.stated_position: Iran demands complete and verifiable sanctions removal before significant nuclear concessions
nodes.batna_description: China-Russia trade and cryptocurrency workarounds provide limited economic relief

nodes.label: Interest
nodes.description: US core interest in preventing Iranian nuclear weapons breakout, which would trigger regional proliferation (Saudi, Turkey, Egypt) and undermine US extended deterrence commitments in the Middle East.
nodes.stated_position: The US will never allow Iran to acquire a nuclear weapon
nodes.batna_description: Military strike option against Iranian nuclear facilities; continued maximum pressure sanctions

nodes.label: Interest
nodes.description: US interest in limiting Iranian regional influence, deterring proxy attacks on US forces and allies, and protecting Israeli and Gulf Arab security.
nodes.stated_position: Iran must cease destabilising regional activities as part of any comprehensive deal
nodes.batna_description: Maintain maximum pressure and deter through forward military presence

nodes.label: Process
nodes.governing_rules: JCPOA text, UN Security Council Resolution 2231 (2015)
nodes.description: The JCPOA negotiation and implementation process: a multilateral framework established in 2015, suspended after US withdrawal in 2018, and subject to restoration attempts through Vienna talks (2021–2022). Currently stalled with UN sanctions snapback in force.

nodes.label: Process
nodes.governing_rules: EU High Representative coordinating indirect US-Iran talks
nodes.description: Vienna restoration talks (2021–2022) facilitated by EU High Representative Enrique Mora. Indirect format (US and Iran not in same room) due to mutual political constraints. Produced near-final text but collapsed over IRGC designation issue.

nodes.label: Outcome
nodes.description: The 2015 JCPOA agreement: Iran reduced enriched uranium stockpile by 98%, limited enrichment to 3.67%, reduced centrifuges to ~6,000, accepted IAEA Additional Protocol, redesigned Arak reactor. In exchange, US and EU lifted nuclear-related sanctions. UN SCRES 2231 endorsed the deal.
terms: Iran limits enrichment to 3.67% for 15 years
Iran reduces enriched uranium stockpile to 300kg
Arak reactor redesigned to prevent weapons-grade plutonium production
IAEA Additional Protocol and enhanced inspections regime
Phased removal of US and EU nuclear-related sanctions
UN arms embargo maintained with sunset clause

nodes.label: Outcome
nodes.description: Post-snapback stalemate: JCPOA is effectively defunct with UN sanctions reimposed. Iran enriching at 60%+ with near-weapons-grade traces at 83.7%. Breakout time assessed at less than two weeks. Diplomatic path to restoration appears blocked by mutual domestic constraints.
terms: All pre-2015 UN sanctions reimposed via snapback
Iran no longer bound by any JCPOA nuclear limits
IAEA Additional Protocol access suspended by Iran
Breakout time at historical minimum (~12 days)
Diplomatic process abandoned with no replacement mechanism

nodes.label: Narrative
nodes.content: Iran has an inalienable right under the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty to peaceful nuclear energy and enrichment. Western pressure is neo-colonial interference designed to deny Iran its sovereign rights and maintain Western technological dominance. Iran is the victim of illegal unilateral sanctions that constitute an act of economic warfare against the Iranian people.
nodes.dominant_frame: Sovereign rights under NPT; anti-imperialism; economic victimhood
nodes.counter_frame: NPT was not designed to legitimise weapons-capable enrichment programs

nodes.label: Narrative
nodes.content: Iran is using a civilian nuclear program as cover for developing nuclear weapons capability. The regime's track record of clandestine activities (Natanz, Fordow, Parchin) demonstrates its deceptive intent. A nuclear-armed Iran would trigger regional proliferation, embolden proxy forces, and constitute an existential threat to Israel. Maximum pressure is necessary to prevent the world's leading state sponsor of terrorism from acquiring the ultimate weapon.
nodes.dominant_frame: Proliferation threat; rogue state; terrorism sponsorship
nodes.counter_frame: Iran has not made the final decision to build a weapon; enrichment is NPT-legitimate
